project.name: Probability for Physician Researchers
project.description: 30-chapter interactive probability textbook expansion (Ch31-60) for the BiostatTextbook Quarto project. Covers foundational through advanced probability with heavy emphasis on interactive Observable JS calculators and visualizations.

integration_notes.quarto_yml_addition: Add a new Part 'Probability' to _quarto.yml after the existing Expert part, before Appendices
integration_notes.numbering: Chapters 31-60 continue numbering from the existing 30 chapters
integration_notes.shared_modules: Reuse existing assets/js/ modules (stat-engine.js, plot-factory.js, etc.) plus new probability-specific modules
new_modules_needed: assets/js/prob-engine.js — combinatorics, distributions, Markov chains, stochastic processes
assets/js/prob-plots.js — probability-specific visualizations (tree diagrams, Venn, urn models, Markov diagrams)
assets/js/simulation-engine.js — Monte Carlo, random walks, convergence animations

phase_1_foundation.description: Create probability-specific OJS modules + new sample datasets

phase_3_integration.description: Merge into main book, cross-reference with existing Ch1-30

foundation.title: Probability OJS Modules & Datasets

features: Combinatorics: factorial, permutations, combinations, multinomial coefficients, Stirling approximation, derangements
Set operations: union, intersection, complement, symmetric difference with probability calculations
Conditional probability: P(A|B) from joint/marginal, chain rule for n events
Bayes calculator: prior × likelihood → posterior, sequential updating for n observations
Discrete distributions: Bernoulli, Binomial, Geometric, Negative Binomial, Poisson, Hypergeometric, Discrete Uniform, Zipf, Multinomial — all with PMF, CDF, quantile, mean, variance, MGF
Continuous distributions: Uniform, Normal, Exponential, Gamma, Beta, Chi-squared, t, F, Weibull, Lognormal, Cauchy, Pareto, Logistic — all with PDF, CDF, quantile, mean, variance, MGF
Multivariate: bivariate Normal PDF/CDF, covariance matrix operations, Mahalanobis distance
Generating functions: PGF, MGF, characteristic function evaluation and inversion
Markov chains: transition matrix powers, stationary distribution, absorption probabilities, mean first passage time, classification (recurrent/transient/absorbing)
Random number generation: LCG, inverse transform, Box-Muller, acceptance-rejection (all visualizable)
Monte Carlo: importance sampling, stratified sampling, antithetic variates, control variates
Convergence: check convergence in probability, distribution, almost sure (via simulation)
Information theory: entropy, KL divergence, mutual information, Fisher information
Venn diagrams: 2-set and 3-set with proportional areas, animated highlighting for operations
Tree diagrams: sequential probability trees with branch probabilities and leaf calculations
Urn models: animated ball drawing with/without replacement, color-coded
Probability tables: joint/marginal/conditional probability tables with highlighting
Distribution comparison: overlay multiple distributions with parameter sliders
QQ plots: any distribution against any other, with confidence bands
Hazard/survival function plots from any lifetime distribution
Markov chain diagrams: state transition graphs with animated probability flow
Random walk visualizations: 1D, 2D, lattice walks with path tracing
Convergence plots: running average, empirical CDF vs theoretical, epsilon-band visualization
Copula visualizations: scatter plots with marginal distributions, dependence structures
Entropy diagrams: information content visualization, Venn diagrams for mutual information

simulation-engine.js.description: Monte Carlo and stochastic simulation framework
features: Configurable simulation runner: n iterations, seed control, progress callback
Animated sample accumulation: watch histogram/density build as samples arrive
Convergence tracker: plot running estimate vs true value with CI bands
Random walk engine: 1D/2D/3D walks with configurable step distributions
Markov chain simulator: run chains, visualize state occupancy over time
Queuing simulator: M/M/1, M/M/c with arrival/service animations
Branching process simulator: generation-by-generation population evolution
Bootstrap engine: resample, compute statistic, build sampling distribution

new_datasets.description: 10,000 sequences of 100 fair coin flips for empirical probability exploration
50,000 rolls of various dice (d4, d6, d8, d12, d20) for discrete distribution work
ED arrival times and service times for Poisson process / exponential / queuing exercises
Population screening data with true disease status, multiple test results, prevalence variation by subgroup
10 years of daily weather state transitions (sunny/cloudy/rainy) for Markov chain exercises
Bivariate clinical measurements (e.g., systolic/diastolic BP, height/weight) for joint distribution and copula work

quality_gates: All three new OJS modules import correctly
prob-engine.js: binomial PMF matches R dbinom() within 1e-12
prob-engine.js: Markov stationary distribution matches R eigen() within 1e-10
prob-plots.js: Venn diagrams render with correct areas
simulation-engine.js: Monte Carlo estimate of π converges
All datasets load in both Julia and WebR

foundation.title: Probability Appendices & Cross-References
tasks: Create appendix/probability-formulas.qmd — all probability formulas organized by chapter (31-60)

Create appendix/distribution-reference.qmd — comprehensive distribution table: name, parameters, PMF/PDF, CDF, mean, variance, MGF, relationships, when to use, clinical examples. Interactive: click any distribution to see plot
Create appendix/probability-tables.qmd — standard Normal table, t-table, chi-squared table, F-table (interactive: enter value, get probability, or enter probability, get critical value)
Create appendix/simulation-cookbook.qmd — OJS code patterns for common probability simulations
Update appendix/glossary.qmd with 200+ probability-specific terms
Add Part 'Probability' to _quarto.yml with all 30 new chapters

chapters_beginner.title: Ch31: What Is Probability?
content: 31.1 Why physicians need probability — clinical reasoning IS probabilistic reasoning
31.2 Three interpretations: Classical (equally likely outcomes), Frequentist (long-run frequency), Bayesian (degree of belief)
31.3 Sample spaces and events — Ω, simple events, compound events, impossible/certain
31.4 Kolmogorov axioms — non-negativity, normalization, countable additivity
31.5 Properties: complement rule, inclusion-exclusion for 2 and 3 events
31.6 Probability as area — geometric probability, Buffon's needle preview
31.7 Common pitfalls: gambler's fallacy, base rate neglect, conjunction fallacy

ojs_demos.title: Coin Flip Laboratory
ojs_demos.description: Flip n coins (slider: 1→10,000). Watch relative frequency converge to 0.5. Real-time running proportion plot. Toggle: fair vs biased coin (slider: p from 0.01 to 0.99). THE foundational demo — probability as long-run frequency.

ojs_demos.title: Dice Probability Calculator
ojs_demos.description: Select dice type (d4/d6/d8/d12/d20) and number of dice. Define event (e.g., 'sum > 7'). Compute exact probability by enumeration. Show sample space as grid. Highlight favorable outcomes.

ojs_demos.title: Venn Diagram Probability Machine
ojs_demos.description: Two or three events. Sliders: P(A), P(B), P(A∩B). Auto-compute P(A∪B), P(Aᶜ), P(A−B). Proportional Venn diagram updates in real-time. Highlight selected region.

ojs_demos.title: Gambler's Fallacy Simulator
ojs_demos.description: Streak detector: after seeing HHHHH, what's P(H) on next flip? Simulate 100,000 sequences. Show that post-streak probability is still p. Bust the myth with data.

ojs_demos.title: Geometric Probability Playground
ojs_demos.description: Drop points randomly into a square. Count proportion landing in inscribed circle. Estimate π. Slider: number of points. Watch convergence.

chapters_beginner.webr_analysis: Upload any binary outcome data → compute empirical probability with exact binomial CI → compare to theoretical if specified

chapters_beginner.title: Ch32: Counting and Combinatorics
content: 32.1 Multiplication principle — sequential choices, tree diagrams
32.2 Permutations — ordered arrangements, n!, P(n,r), with repetition
32.3 Combinations — unordered selections, C(n,r), Pascal's triangle
32.4 Multinomial coefficients — distributing objects into groups
32.5 Stars and bars — distributing identical objects
32.6 Clinical applications: how many ways to assign treatments, genetic combinations, diagnostic test orderings
32.7 Stirling's approximation — handling large factorials
32.8 The birthday problem — surprising collision probabilities

ojs_demos.title: Permutation vs Combination Calculator
ojs_demos.description: Input n and r. Toggle order matters/doesn't. Show P(n,r) vs C(n,r) with visual: arranging colored balls in slots. See why C < P.

ojs_demos.title: Pascal's Triangle Explorer
ojs_demos.description: Interactive Pascal's triangle (up to row 20). Click any entry to see the combination it represents. Highlight diagonals (natural numbers, triangular numbers, Fibonacci). Color by divisibility.

ojs_demos.title: Birthday Problem Simulator
ojs_demos.description: Slider: number of people (2→100). Show exact P(at least one shared birthday). Run simulation (1000 trials). Compare theoretical vs empirical. Show the surprising threshold (~23 people for 50%).

ojs_demos.title: Treatment Assignment Counter
ojs_demos.description: Clinical scenario: n patients, k treatment arms, block sizes. Compute number of possible randomization sequences. Show why balance matters.

ojs_demos.title: Lottery/Rare Event Calculator
ojs_demos.description: Input: total items, items to choose, winning items. Compute P(winning). Show on log scale. Compare to clinical risks (MI, adverse drug reaction) for perspective.

chapters_beginner.webr_analysis: Combinatorial probability calculator: input scenario parameters → compute exact probability using R's choose() and factorial()

chapters_beginner.title: Ch33: Conditional Probability and Independence
content: 33.1 Conditional probability P(A|B) — definition, intuition as 'updating the sample space'
33.2 Multiplication rule — P(A∩B) = P(A|B)P(B), chain rule for n events
33.3 Independence — P(A|B) = P(A), mutual vs pairwise independence
33.4 Law of Total Probability — partitioning the sample space
33.5 Bayes' theorem — derivation, prior→posterior, clinical diagnostic reasoning
33.6 Sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV as conditional probabilities
33.7 Sequential testing — updating beliefs with multiple tests
33.8 Simpson's paradox — when conditional and marginal probabilities conflict
33.9 Common errors: confusing P(A|B) with P(B|A), prosecutor's fallacy

ojs_demos.title: Conditional Probability Table Machine
ojs_demos.description: Input joint probability table (2×2 or 3×3). Auto-compute all marginal and conditional probabilities. Highlight selected conditional. Animated Venn diagram shows the 'zooming in' intuition of conditioning.

ojs_demos.title: Bayes' Theorem Step-by-Step
ojs_demos.description: Clinical scenario: disease prevalence (slider), test sensitivity (slider), test specificity (slider). Walk through Bayes computation step by step. Show natural frequency tree (1000 patients). Show Fagan nomogram. Show icon array (TP/FP/TN/FN colored dots). THE must-have clinical probability demo.

ojs_demos.title: Independence Tester
ojs_demos.description: Input 2×2 table of counts. Compute P(A), P(B), P(A∩B), P(A)×P(B). Visual comparison: if equal → independent. Show chi-squared test as formal check. Animated: generate random independent vs dependent data.

ojs_demos.title: Sequential Diagnostic Testing
ojs_demos.description: Start with pre-test probability. Add Test 1 (sens/spec) → post-test probability becomes pre-test for Test 2. Chain up to 5 tests. Waterfall chart showing probability evolution. Show when additional tests add diminishing value.

ojs_demos.title: Simpson's Paradox Demonstrator
ojs_demos.description: The kidney stone treatment example. Show that Treatment A wins overall but Treatment B wins in every subgroup. Interactive: adjust subgroup sizes to create/eliminate the paradox. DAG showing the confounding structure.

ojs_demos.title: Prosecutor's Fallacy Explainer
ojs_demos.description: P(evidence|innocent) ≠ P(innocent|evidence). Input match probability, prior probability of guilt. Show how Bayes' theorem gives the correct answer. Use the Sally Clark case as example.

chapters_beginner.webr_analysis: Upload diagnostic data → full Bayesian analysis: prevalence estimation, test accuracy metrics, sequential test evaluation, subgroup-stratified analysis checking for Simpson's paradox

chapters_beginner.title: Ch34: Discrete Random Variables
content: 34.1 What is a random variable? — mapping outcomes to numbers
34.2 Probability mass function (PMF) — properties, visualization
34.3 Cumulative distribution function (CDF) — step function, properties
34.4 Expected value E(X) — definition, computation, linearity, clinical interpretation as 'average outcome'
34.5 Variance and standard deviation — Var(X) = E(X²) - [E(X)]², properties
34.6 Bernoulli distribution — single trial, p, building block for everything
34.7 Binomial distribution — n trials, exact and approximate calculations, clinical: treatment responders
34.8 Geometric distribution — waiting for first success, memoryless property
34.9 Negative Binomial — waiting for r-th success, overdispersed counts
34.10 Poisson distribution — rare events, approximation to Binomial, clinical: adverse events per patient-year
34.11 Hypergeometric — sampling without replacement, Fisher's exact test connection

ojs_demos.title: PMF/CDF Builder
ojs_demos.description: Define a custom PMF by dragging bar heights. Auto-normalize. Show corresponding CDF (step function) side by side. Compute E(X), Var(X), SD(X) live. Toggle: show E(X) as balance point.

ojs_demos.title: Binomial Laboratory
ojs_demos.description: Sliders: n (1→200), p (0→1). Show PMF bars + Normal approximation overlay. Show CDF. Compute P(X=k), P(X≤k), P(X≥k) for user-specified k. Highlight: mode, mean, ±1SD region. Clinical framing: 'If a drug works in 30% of patients and you treat 50, what's P(at least 20 respond)?'

ojs_demos.title: Poisson Event Counter
ojs_demos.description: Slider: λ (0.1→20). Show PMF. Simulate Poisson process on a timeline (events as dots). Count events in intervals. Show empirical vs theoretical PMF. Clinical: adverse events per 100 patient-days.

ojs_demos.title: Binomial vs Poisson Comparison
ojs_demos.description: Show when Poisson approximates Binomial (large n, small p). Sliders: n, p. Side-by-side PMFs. Compute max absolute difference. Show convergence as n→∞, p→0, np→λ.

ojs_demos.title: Expected Value Game
ojs_demos.description: Play a clinical decision game. Two treatments: Treatment A has outcome distribution DA, Treatment B has DB. Compute E(X) for each. Choose. Simulate 1000 patients. Show that E(X)-maximizing strategy wins long-run. Introduce risk aversion with utility functions.

ojs_demos.title: Hypergeometric Fisher's Exact
ojs_demos.description: Urn model: N balls, K red, draw n. Slider for each. Show PMF. Connect to 2×2 table and Fisher's exact test. Compute p-value by summing tail probabilities.

chapters_beginner.webr_analysis: Upload count data → auto-fit Poisson, Binomial, Negative Binomial → goodness-of-fit tests → parameter estimation with CIs → overdispersion check

chapters_beginner.title: Ch35: Continuous Random Variables
content: 35.1 From discrete to continuous — why P(X=x) = 0, probability as area
35.2 Probability density function (PDF) — properties, interpretation, not a probability
35.3 CDF for continuous variables — F(x) = P(X≤x), relationship to PDF via integration
35.4 Expected value and variance — integrals replace sums
35.5 Uniform distribution — simplest continuous, simulation foundation
35.6 Exponential distribution — memoryless property, waiting times between Poisson events, hazard rate
35.7 Normal distribution deep dive — parameters μ, σ², the 68-95-99.7 rule, standard Normal Z
35.8 Normal probability calculations — Z-tables, complement, between two values
35.9 Gamma distribution — sum of exponentials, flexible shape, clinical: hospital LOS
35.10 Beta distribution — probabilities of probabilities, conjugate prior for Binomial
35.11 Log-Normal — when logarithm is Normal, clinical: lab values, drug concentrations

ojs_demos.title: PDF Area Calculator
ojs_demos.description: Select distribution. Parameter sliders. Click/drag to shade region under PDF curve. Shows P(a < X < b) as shaded area in real-time. Toggle: show CDF alongside, highlight corresponding CDF segment.

ojs_demos.title: Normal Distribution Workbench
ojs_demos.description: Sliders: μ, σ. Shade modes: P(X < a), P(X > a), P(a < X < b), P(|X-μ| < kσ). Show exact probability. Show Z-score. Draw 68-95-99.7 bands. Toggle: show standard Normal alongside with Z-axis. THE workhorse Normal demo.

ojs_demos.title: Exponential Memoryless Demo
ojs_demos.description: Exponential with rate λ. Show that P(X > s+t | X > s) = P(X > t). Visual: survival function. Slider: conditioning time s. Animate the 'fresh start' property. Clinical: if a patient has waited t minutes, expected additional wait is still 1/λ.

ojs_demos.title: Distribution Shape Explorer
ojs_demos.description: Dropdown: Uniform / Normal / Exponential / Gamma / Beta / Log-Normal / Weibull. Parameter sliders for each. Show PDF, CDF, mean line, median line, mode line. Display moments. Compare any two distributions side by side.

ojs_demos.title: Quantile/Percentile Calculator
ojs_demos.description: Input: distribution + parameters + probability p. Output: quantile x such that P(X ≤ x) = p. Or reverse: input x, get P(X ≤ x). Visual: shaded area up to x on PDF. Clinical: 'What BMI is at the 95th percentile?'

ojs_demos.title: Beta Distribution for Proportions
ojs_demos.description: Sliders: α, β. Show PDF shape morphing (U-shaped, uniform, unimodal, J-shaped). Show how Beta is the conjugate prior for Binomial. Add data: observe k successes in n trials → posterior Beta(α+k, β+n-k). Animated updating.

chapters_beginner.webr_analysis: Upload continuous measurement data → fit multiple distributions (Normal, Log-Normal, Gamma, Weibull) → AIC comparison → QQ plots → goodness-of-fit tests → parameter estimates with CIs

chapters_beginner.title: Ch36: Functions of Random Variables and Transformations
content: 36.1 Functions of a random variable — if X has distribution f, what is the distribution of g(X)?
36.2 The CDF method — F_Y(y) = P(g(X) ≤ y)
36.3 The transformation formula (change of variables) — f_Y(y) = f_X(g⁻¹(y)) |dg⁻¹/dy|
36.4 Linear transformations — Y = aX + b, effect on mean and variance
36.5 Log transformation — why it normalizes right-skewed clinical data
36.6 Box-Cox transformations — finding the optimal power transformation
36.7 Z-score standardization — (X - μ)/σ, universal scale
36.8 Probability integral transform — F(X) ~ Uniform(0,1), foundation of simulation
36.9 Inverse transform sampling — generating random variables from any distribution
36.10 Clinical applications: reference ranges, percentile curves, normalized lab values

ojs_demos.title: Transformation Visualizer
ojs_demos.description: Left panel: PDF of X. Middle panel: transformation function g(x) (user selects: linear, square, log, exp, sqrt, reciprocal, or custom). Right panel: resulting PDF of Y = g(X). Animate a sample from X through the transformation to Y.

ojs_demos.title: Log-Transform Demo
ojs_demos.description: Generate right-skewed data (e.g., Lognormal). Show histogram before and after log transform. Show QQ plots before and after. Compute skewness before and after. Clinical: lab values, drug concentrations, hospital costs.

ojs_demos.title: Z-Score Machine
ojs_demos.description: Input: raw value, mean, SD. Output: Z-score. Visual: mark position on original distribution and standard Normal simultaneously. Slider: sweep through raw values, watch Z-score update. Batch mode: paste column of values, get Z-scores.

ojs_demos.title: Inverse Transform Sampler
ojs_demos.description: Step 1: Show CDF of target distribution. Step 2: Draw U ~ Uniform(0,1) (horizontal line). Step 3: Trace to CDF, drop down to x-axis = generated sample. Animate repeatedly. Watch histogram of generated samples match target PDF. This is HOW random number generators work.

ojs_demos.title: Box-Cox Optimizer
ojs_demos.description: Upload or generate skewed data. Slider: λ from -2 to 2. Show transformed histogram. Compute normality test p-value at each λ. Find optimal λ. Show log-likelihood profile.

chapters_beginner.webr_analysis: Upload data → apply common transformations (log, sqrt, Box-Cox) → compare normality before/after → recommend best transformation → compute back-transformed CIs

types: choose optimal transformation for clinical data

chapters_intermediate.title: Ch37: Joint Distributions and Bivariate Probability
content: 37.1 Joint PMF and PDF — definition, visualization as surfaces/heatmaps
37.2 Marginal distributions — summing/integrating out one variable
37.3 Conditional distributions — f(y|x), conditional expectation E(Y|X=x)
37.4 Independence — f(x,y) = f(x)f(y), testing independence
37.5 Covariance and correlation — Cov(X,Y) = E(XY) - E(X)E(Y), ρ
37.6 Bivariate Normal distribution — parameters, contour plots, conditional distributions
37.7 Multinomial distribution — extension of Binomial to k categories
37.8 Clinical application: joint modeling of systolic/diastolic BP, correlated biomarkers

ojs_demos.title: Joint Distribution Heatmap
ojs_demos.description: 2D heatmap of joint PDF/PMF. Sliders for distribution parameters. Click row/column to show marginal. Click cell to show conditional. Toggle: contour plot vs surface plot vs heatmap.

ojs_demos.title: Bivariate Normal Explorer
ojs_demos.description: Sliders: μ₁, μ₂, σ₁, σ₂, ρ. Show contour ellipses, marginal distributions on axes, conditional distribution for any fixed x. Watch ellipse rotate as ρ changes. Mark P(X>a, Y>b) as shaded region.

ojs_demos.title: Covariance/Correlation Builder
ojs_demos.description: Scatter plot of bivariate data. Drag points to adjust. Watch Cov(X,Y) and ρ update live. Show the deviation rectangles that define covariance. Toggle: show regression line.

ojs_demos.title: Independence Checker
ojs_demos.description: Input joint probability table (up to 4×4). Compute all marginals. Check if f(x,y) = f(x)f(y) for every cell. Highlight violations. Compute chi-squared test.

ojs_demos.title: Marginal vs Conditional
ojs_demos.description: Joint distribution of (Disease Severity, Treatment Response). Show marginal of Response. Then condition on Severity=Mild, Moderate, Severe. Show how conditional distributions differ from marginal. Animate the 'slicing' operation.

chapters_intermediate.webr_analysis: Upload bivariate data → scatterplot + marginal histograms → correlation + covariance with CIs → test for bivariate normality → conditional distribution analysis

chapters_intermediate.title: Ch38: Sums of Random Variables and Convolutions
content: 38.1 Distribution of X+Y — convolution of PDFs/PMFs
38.2 Sum of independent Normals — still Normal, parameters add simply
38.3 Sum of independent Poissons — still Poisson, rates add
38.4 Sum of independent Exponentials — Gamma distribution
38.5 E(X+Y) = E(X) + E(Y) always; Var(X+Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y) only if independent
38.6 Distribution of X̄ — the sampling distribution derived properly
38.7 Chi-squared as sum of squared standard Normals
38.8 Clinical: total dose = sum of individual doses, aggregate costs, combined risk scores

ojs_demos.title: Convolution Visualizer
ojs_demos.description: Select two distributions. Show individual PDFs. Animate the convolution process: slide one PDF across the other, show multiplication area at each point. Result: PDF of sum. Stunning visual demonstration of convolution.

ojs_demos.title: Sum of Normals
ojs_demos.description: Two Normal distributions with adjustable μ and σ. Show individual PDFs. Show PDF of sum (also Normal). Verify: μ_sum = μ₁+μ₂, σ²_sum = σ₁²+σ₂². Extend to sum of 3, 4, 5 Normals.

ojs_demos.title: Exponential to Gamma
ojs_demos.description: Sum of n Exponential(λ). Slider: n from 1 to 20. Show Erlang/Gamma PDF emerging. Animate: roll dice-like exponentials, accumulate sum, build histogram, compare to Gamma(n, λ).

ojs_demos.title: Variance of Sums
ojs_demos.description: Two random variables with adjustable correlation ρ. Show Var(X+Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y) + 2Cov(X,Y). Slider: ρ from -1 to 1. Watch variance of sum change. Show why diversification reduces portfolio risk (negative correlation).

chapters_intermediate.title: Ch39: Moment Generating Functions and Characteristic Functions
content: 39.1 Moments — E(X), E(X²), E(Xᵏ), central moments, standardized moments
39.2 Moment generating function — M(t) = E(e^{tX}), properties, uniqueness
39.3 Using MGF to find moments — M'(0) = E(X), M''(0) = E(X²), etc.
39.4 MGF of common distributions — Bernoulli, Binomial, Poisson, Normal, Exponential, Gamma
39.5 MGF of sums of independent RVs — M_{X+Y}(t) = M_X(t)·M_Y(t)
39.6 Probability generating function — G(s) = E(s^X) for non-negative integers
39.7 Characteristic function — φ(t) = E(e^{itX}), always exists, Fourier transform connection
39.8 Cumulant generating function — log MGF, cumulants, why Normal has only 2 non-zero cumulants

ojs_demos.title: MGF Explorer
ojs_demos.description: Select distribution. Show MGF formula and plot M(t) vs t. Compute derivatives at t=0 to extract moments. Verify: M'(0) = E(X), M''(0) = E(X²). Show moment recovery step by step.

ojs_demos.title: MGF Product = Sum
ojs_demos.description: Two independent distributions. Show individual MGFs. Multiply them → get MGF of sum. Identify the resulting distribution. Example: Binomial(n₁,p) + Binomial(n₂,p) = Binomial(n₁+n₂,p) via MGF.

ojs_demos.title: Moment Calculator
ojs_demos.description: Input distribution + parameters. Compute and display: mean, variance, skewness, kurtosis (both raw and central moments). Show formulas. Compare moments across distribution families.

ojs_demos.title: Characteristic Function Visualizer
ojs_demos.description: Show φ(t) in the complex plane (real and imaginary parts). For Normal: φ(t) = exp(iμt - σ²t²/2). Animate for different distributions. Show why CF always exists (even when MGF doesn't, e.g., Cauchy).

chapters_intermediate.title: Ch40: Inequalities and Bounds
content: 40.1 Why bounds matter — when exact distributions are unknown or intractable
40.2 Markov's inequality — P(X ≥ a) ≤ E(X)/a, non-negative RVs only
40.3 Chebyshev's inequality — P(|X-μ| ≥ kσ) ≤ 1/k², any distribution
40.4 Comparing Chebyshev to exact (Normal: much tighter than Chebyshev bound)
40.5 Chernoff bounds — exponential tail bounds via MGF, tightest of the three
40.6 Jensen's inequality — E(g(X)) ≥ g(E(X)) for convex g, applications
40.7 Cauchy-Schwarz inequality — |E(XY)|² ≤ E(X²)E(Y²), correlation bounded by ±1
40.8 Clinical applications: worst-case risk bounds, outlier probability bounds

ojs_demos.title: Inequality Comparison Dashboard
ojs_demos.description: Select distribution. Input threshold a. Compute exact P(X ≥ a), Markov bound, Chebyshev bound, Chernoff bound. Bar chart comparing all four. Shows how much looser the bounds are. Slider: a from 1 to 5 standard deviations.

ojs_demos.title: Chebyshev vs Reality
ojs_demos.description: Select distribution (Normal, Uniform, Exponential, etc.). Shade P(|X-μ| ≥ kσ). Compare exact probability to Chebyshev bound 1/k². Show that Chebyshev is conservative but always valid. Slider: k from 1 to 5.

ojs_demos.title: Jensen's Inequality Visualizer
ojs_demos.description: Draw a convex function g(x). Show distribution of X. Mark E(X) on x-axis. Show g(E(X)) vs E(g(X)). Illustrate: the average of the function ≥ the function of the average. Clinical: why average cost ≠ cost at average patient.

chapters_intermediate.title: Ch41: The Law of Large Numbers
content: 41.1 Weak Law of Large Numbers — convergence in probability of X̄ to μ
41.2 Proof via Chebyshev — Var(X̄) = σ²/n → 0
41.3 Strong Law of Large Numbers — almost sure convergence
41.4 What LLN does NOT say — it doesn't say individual observations converge
41.5 Rate of convergence — √n rate, connection to standard error
41.6 LLN for proportions — p̂ → p, foundation of frequentist probability
41.7 Clinical applications: why large RCTs are more reliable, insurance pooling, why small samples are dangerous

ojs_demos.title: LLN Live Simulator
ojs_demos.description: Select distribution with known μ. Generate observations one at a time (or in batches). Plot running average X̄_n vs n. Show convergence to μ. Add ε-band: P(|X̄_n - μ| < ε). Multiple simultaneous paths (5-10 sample paths, all converging). THE core LLN visualization.

ojs_demos.title: Sample Size and Precision
ojs_demos.description: Show how X̄ gets more precise as n grows. Plot distribution of X̄ for n = 5, 10, 50, 100, 500, 1000. Watch it tighten around μ. Show that SD(X̄) = σ/√n. The visual argument for why larger studies are better.

ojs_demos.title: LLN Failure Cases
ojs_demos.description: Show what happens with heavy-tailed distributions (Cauchy — no mean, LLN doesn't apply). X̄ does NOT converge. Compare: Normal (converges beautifully) vs Cauchy (wanders forever). Powerful lesson.

ojs_demos.title: Insurance Pooling Demo
ojs_demos.description: n policyholders, each with random claim X. Premium set at E(X) + margin. Show that as n increases, P(total claims exceed total premiums) → 0. The mathematical foundation of insurance.

chapters_intermediate.title: Ch42: Central Limit Theorem — The Deep Dive
content: 42.1 Statement of CLT — (X̄ - μ)/(σ/√n) → N(0,1) in distribution
42.2 Why CLT is the most important theorem in statistics — justifies t-tests, CIs, z-tests
42.3 Visual demonstration: sum of Uniforms → Normal, sum of Exponentials → Normal
42.4 Rate of convergence — Berry-Esseen theorem, when n=30 is enough (and when it isn't)
42.5 CLT for proportions — p̂ ≈ N(p, p(1-p)/n), normal approximation to Binomial
42.6 CLT for sums — S_n ≈ N(nμ, nσ²), total costs/doses/events
42.7 Continuity correction — for discrete → continuous approximation
42.8 Delta method — approximate distribution of g(X̄) for smooth g
42.9 Multivariate CLT — X̄ → N(μ, Σ/n) in multiple dimensions
42.10 When CLT fails — heavy tails, infinite variance, non-iid samples

ojs_demos.title: Ultimate CLT Simulator
ojs_demos.description: Choose ANY source distribution: Uniform, Exponential, Bernoulli, Poisson, Chi-squared, custom. Slider: sample size n (1→500). Generate 10,000 sample means. Show histogram morphing from source shape to bell curve as n increases. Overlay Normal PDF. Show QQ plot. Compute KS test p-value against Normal. Animated version: watch it happen frame by frame as n increases. THE definitive CLT demo.

ojs_demos.title: Berry-Esseen Convergence Rate
ojs_demos.description: Select distribution with known skewness γ. Show exact CDF of X̄ vs Normal CDF. Show Berry-Esseen bound on maximum difference. Demonstrate: highly skewed distributions need larger n. How large is 'large enough'? Clinical: when is n=30 adequate?

ojs_demos.title: Normal Approximation to Binomial
ojs_demos.description: Sliders: n, p. Show exact Binomial PMF. Overlay Normal approximation PDF. Show with and without continuity correction. Compute max error. Show when approximation breaks down (p near 0 or 1, small n).

ojs_demos.title: Delta Method Calculator
ojs_demos.description: Function g(x) (e.g., log, exp, 1/x, x²). Input distribution of X̄. Compute approximate distribution of g(X̄) via Delta Method. Compare to exact (via simulation). Clinical: CI for odds ratio from log-OR.

chapters_intermediate.webr_analysis: Upload data → test CLT assumption: is sample mean approximately Normal? → QQ plot of bootstrap means → Shapiro-Wilk on resampled means → recommend: parametric OK or use nonparametric

chapters_intermediate.title: Ch43: Order Statistics and Extreme Values
content: 43.1 Order statistics X₍₁₎ ≤ X₍₂₎ ≤ ... ≤ X₍ₙ₎ — the sorted sample
43.2 Distribution of X₍k₎ — PDF and CDF of the k-th order statistic
43.3 Distribution of minimum X₍₁₎ and maximum X₍ₙ₎ — tail behavior
43.4 Range R = X₍ₙ₎ - X₍₁₎, midrange, interquartile range as order statistics
43.5 Sample median and quantiles as order statistics
43.6 Extreme value theory — Gumbel, Fréchet, Weibull types
43.7 Fisher-Tippett theorem — the CLT for maxima
43.8 Clinical: reference range construction, outlier detection, extreme lab values, worst-case outcomes

ojs_demos.title: Order Statistic Explorer
ojs_demos.description: Generate n samples from selected distribution. Show sorted values on number line. Highlight k-th order statistic. Show theoretical PDF of X₍k₎. Slider: k from 1 to n. Show how median = X₍(n+1)/2₎.

ojs_demos.title: Min/Max Distribution
ojs_demos.description: n iid samples from distribution. Show PDF of min and max. As n increases, min shifts left, max shifts right. Show why max of Normals grows like √(2 log n). Clinical: worst case in a group of n patients.

ojs_demos.title: Extreme Value Type Selector
ojs_demos.description: Generate maxima of samples of size n from various distributions. Fit GEV distribution. Show which type (Gumbel/Fréchet/Weibull) applies. Clinical: modeling extreme lab values, 100-year flood equivalent in clinical data.

chapters_intermediate.title: Ch44: Convergence Concepts
content: 44.1 Four modes of convergence — almost sure, in probability, in distribution, in mean square
44.2 Relationships: a.s. → in probability → in distribution; in mean square → in probability
44.3 Convergence in distribution — CDF convergence, Portmanteau theorem
44.4 Slutsky's theorem — if X_n →d X and Y_n →p c, then X_n + Y_n →d X + c
44.5 Continuous mapping theorem — if X_n →d X, then g(X_n) →d g(X) for continuous g
44.6 Why this matters for statistics — justifies plug-in estimators, bootstrap, asymptotics
44.7 Clinical: convergence of treatment effect estimates as trial enrolls patients

ojs_demos.title: Four Modes of Convergence
ojs_demos.description: Visual comparison. Same sequence X_n, show all four convergence checks. Highlight which modes hold and which don't for specific examples. E.g., convergence in distribution but not in probability.

ojs_demos.title: Convergence Speed Comparison
ojs_demos.description: Three estimators of the same parameter. Run n = 10, 50, 100, 500, 1000. Compare convergence rates. Show MSE vs n. Which estimator converges fastest?

ojs_demos.title: Slutsky's Theorem in Action
ojs_demos.description: X_n →d N(0,1), Y_n = σ̂/σ →p 1. Show that X_n/Y_n →d N(0,1). This is why t-statistics work! Animate: as n grows, the ratio distribution approaches standard Normal.

chapters_intermediate.title: Ch45: Conditional Expectation and Prediction
content: 45.1 Conditional expectation E(Y|X=x) — the regression function
45.2 Law of iterated expectation — E(Y) = E[E(Y|X)], the tower property
45.3 Law of total variance — Var(Y) = E[Var(Y|X)] + Var[E(Y|X)]
45.4 Conditional expectation as best predictor — minimizes MSE
45.5 Linear prediction — E(Y|X) when joint is bivariate Normal
45.6 Connection to regression — E(Y|X=x) = α + βx for bivariate Normal
45.7 Adam's law and Eve's law (iterated expectation/variance) in clinical research
45.8 Clinical: risk stratification, subgroup analysis justification, variance decomposition in hierarchical data

ojs_demos.title: Conditional Expectation Visualizer
ojs_demos.description: Bivariate scatter plot. Draw E(Y|X=x) curve through the data (regression function). Show it's the curve that minimizes MSE. Compare to: best constant predictor E(Y), best linear predictor, true conditional mean.

ojs_demos.title: Law of Total Expectation
ojs_demos.description: Population divided into subgroups. Show E(Y) in each subgroup. Weighted average = E(Y) overall. Adjust subgroup sizes/means. Show the decomposition in action. Clinical: overall treatment effect from subgroup effects.

ojs_demos.title: Variance Decomposition
ojs_demos.description: Show Var(Y) = E[Var(Y|X)] + Var[E(Y|X)]. The 'within-group variance' + 'between-group variance'. Slider: adjust group separation. When between > within → groups are meaningfully different. Foundation of ANOVA, ICC.

chapters_advanced.title: Ch46: Multivariate Distributions
content: 46.1 Multivariate Normal — μ vector, Σ covariance matrix, PDF, contour ellipsoids
46.2 Properties: marginals are Normal, conditionals are Normal, linear combinations are Normal
46.3 Mahalanobis distance — (x-μ)ᵀΣ⁻¹(x-μ), multivariate outlier detection
46.4 Wishart distribution — distribution of sample covariance matrix
46.5 Dirichlet distribution — multivariate generalization of Beta, compositional data
46.6 Copulas — modeling dependence separately from marginals
46.7 Clinical: multivariate reference ranges, correlated biomarker panels, compositional microbiome data

ojs_demos.title: Multivariate Normal 3D Explorer
ojs_demos.description: 3D surface plot of bivariate Normal PDF. Rotate/zoom. Sliders: μ₁, μ₂, σ₁, σ₂, ρ. Show contour ellipses at 1σ, 2σ, 3σ. Click any point to see Mahalanobis distance.

ojs_demos.title: Copula Gallery
ojs_demos.description: Same marginals (e.g., both Normal), different copulas (Gaussian, Clayton, Gumbel, Frank). Show scatter plots and contour plots. Demonstrate that dependence structure is separate from marginals.

ojs_demos.title: Mahalanobis Outlier Detector
ojs_demos.description: Upload or generate bivariate data. Compute Mahalanobis distances. Color points by distance. Mark outliers beyond chi-squared threshold. Compare to univariate outlier detection.

chapters_advanced.title: Ch47: Stochastic Processes — Introduction
content: 47.1 What is a stochastic process? — {X(t), t ∈ T} or {X_n, n ∈ ℕ}
47.2 Classification: discrete/continuous time × discrete/continuous state
47.3 Bernoulli process — sequence of independent trials, foundation
47.4 Random walks — simple, with drift, on integers, gambler's ruin
47.5 Poisson process — counting events in time, properties (independent/stationary increments)
47.6 Inter-arrival and waiting times — Exponential and Gamma connections
47.7 Non-homogeneous Poisson process — time-varying rate λ(t)
47.8 Clinical: patient arrivals to ED, adverse event monitoring, sequential trial monitoring

ojs_demos.title: Random Walk Simulator
ojs_demos.description: 1D random walk: step +1 or -1 with probability p/q. Animate path. Slider: p from 0 to 1. Show: with p=0.5 walk is recurrent (returns to origin), with p≠0.5 drifts away. Multiple simultaneous walks. Show gambler's ruin probability.

ojs_demos.title: 2D Random Walk
ojs_demos.description: Walk on integer lattice. Animated path tracing. Show visited sites heatmap. Demonstrate recurrence in 2D (returns to origin with probability 1) vs transience in 3D.

ojs_demos.title: Poisson Process Animator
ojs_demos.description: Events on a timeline. Slider: rate λ. Watch events appear. Count events in intervals. Show that counts follow Poisson(λt). Show inter-arrival times follow Exp(λ). Superpose two processes. Clinical: ED arrivals.

ojs_demos.title: Gambler's Ruin Calculator
ojs_demos.description: Player starts with $a, opponent has $(b-a). Each round: win/lose $1 with probability p/q. Compute: P(ruin), E(game duration). Slider: a, b, p. Run simulations. Show that even near-fair games end in ruin given enough time.

chapters_advanced.title: Ch48: Markov Chains
content: 48.1 Markov property — the future depends only on the present, not the past
48.2 Transition matrix P — P_{ij} = P(X_{n+1}=j | X_n=i)
48.3 n-step transition probabilities — P^n = P × P × ... × P
48.4 State classification — recurrent, transient, absorbing, communicating classes
48.5 Periodicity — aperiodic vs periodic chains
48.6 Stationary distribution — π = πP, existence and uniqueness, ergodic theorem
48.7 Absorption probabilities — reaching absorbing states, mean time to absorption
48.8 Clinical: disease progression models, patient flow through hospital, Markov models in health economics (cost-effectiveness)

ojs_demos.title: Markov Chain Builder
ojs_demos.description: Draw states as circles. Draw arrows with transition probabilities (must sum to 1 from each state). Animate: token moves through states according to probabilities. Show state occupancy histogram building up. Compute stationary distribution.

ojs_demos.title: Disease Progression Model
ojs_demos.description: States: Healthy → Mild → Moderate → Severe → Dead. Input transition probabilities. Run cohort of 1000 patients forward in time. Show population distribution across states at each time step. Compute expected time in each state. Life expectancy. QALY calculation.

ojs_demos.title: Transition Matrix Powers
ojs_demos.description: Input transition matrix (up to 5×5). Show P, P², P³, ..., P^n. Watch rows converge to stationary distribution. Visualize convergence rate. Show eigenvalue decomposition.

ojs_demos.title: Absorbing Chain Calculator
ojs_demos.description: Mark states as absorbing or transient. Compute fundamental matrix N = (I-Q)⁻¹. Show absorption probabilities B = NR. Show expected number of visits to each transient state. Mean time to absorption.

ojs_demos.title: PageRank Demo
ojs_demos.description: Small web graph (5-10 pages). Links as directed edges. Compute PageRank via Markov chain. Show stationary distribution = page importance. Add/remove links, watch rankings change. The Google algorithm explained.

chapters_advanced.webr_analysis: Upload longitudinal state data → estimate transition matrix → classify states → compute stationary distribution → project forward → cost-effectiveness analysis

chapters_advanced.title: Ch49: Poisson Processes and Renewal Theory
content: 49.1 Poisson process formal definition — three equivalent definitions
49.2 Merging and splitting — superposition of independent Poisson processes
49.3 Conditional distribution of arrival times given N(t) = n — uniform on [0,t]
49.4 Non-homogeneous Poisson processes — time-varying rate λ(t), thinning
49.5 Compound Poisson process — random sizes at Poisson-arriving events
49.6 Renewal processes — generalize Poisson by allowing non-exponential inter-arrivals
49.7 Elementary renewal theorem — N(t)/t → 1/E(X) as t→∞
49.8 Clinical: modeling adverse event arrivals, ED patient flow, equipment failures, disease recurrences

ojs_demos.title: Poisson Merging/Splitting
ojs_demos.description: Two independent Poisson processes (different rates, different colors). Merge them: result is Poisson with summed rate. Split a process: each event randomly assigned to type A or B. Show that both marginals are Poisson.

ojs_demos.title: Non-Homogeneous Poisson Process
ojs_demos.description: Draw rate function λ(t) by clicking control points. Generate events via thinning algorithm. Show events cluster where rate is high. Clinical: ED arrivals peak at certain hours.

ojs_demos.title: Compound Poisson
ojs_demos.description: Events arrive as Poisson. Each event has a random size (claim amount / cost). Show total accumulated by time t. Compute E[S(t)] and Var[S(t)]. Insurance/healthcare cost modeling.

chapters_advanced.title: Ch50: Continuous-Time Markov Chains
content: 50.1 From discrete to continuous time — exponential holding times
50.2 Generator matrix Q — transition rates, Q_{ij} for i≠j, Q_{ii} = -Σ Q_{ij}
50.3 Kolmogorov equations — forward and backward, P'(t) = P(t)Q
50.4 Stationary distribution — πQ = 0
50.5 Birth-death processes — special case with transitions only to neighbors
50.6 Queuing theory basics — M/M/1, M/M/c, Little's law (L = λW)
50.7 Clinical: hospital bed occupancy, ICU patient flow, staffing models

ojs_demos.title: Birth-Death Process Simulator
ojs_demos.description: States 0, 1, 2, ..., N (e.g., number of patients in unit). Birth rates λ_i, death rates μ_i. Animate population over time. Show stationary distribution. Compute utilization ρ = λ/μ.

ojs_demos.title: M/M/1 Queue Simulator
ojs_demos.description: Arrival rate λ, service rate μ. Animate: patients arrive, join queue, get served, leave. Show queue length over time. Show waiting time distribution. Compute L, W, L_q, W_q. Show what happens as ρ → 1 (explosion).

ojs_demos.title: Hospital Bed Planner
ojs_demos.description: Input: admission rate, average LOS, number of beds. Model as M/M/c queue. Compute: occupancy rate, probability of all beds full, expected waiting time for bed. Slider: number of beds to find optimal staffing.

chapters_advanced.title: Ch51: Martingales — Fair Games and Sequential Analysis
content: 51.1 Definition — E(X_{n+1} | X_1,...,X_n) = X_n (conditional expectation equals current value)
51.2 Examples: random walk (fair game), partial sums of mean-zero RVs, likelihood ratios
51.3 Submartingales and supermartingales — E(X_{n+1}|...) ≥ X_n or ≤ X_n
51.4 Optional stopping theorem — expected value preserved at stopping times (with conditions)
51.5 Doob's martingale convergence theorem — bounded martingales converge a.s.
51.6 Applications to sequential testing — SPRT as martingale, Wald's equation
51.7 Clinical: interim analysis stopping rules, group sequential trials, the mathematical foundation of Data Safety Monitoring Boards

ojs_demos.title: Martingale Visualizer
ojs_demos.description: Simulate a martingale (fair random walk). Show multiple paths. Compute running conditional expectation. Verify E(X_{n+1}|X_n) = X_n by simulation. Compare: submartingale (favorable game), supermartingale (unfavorable).

ojs_demos.title: Optional Stopping
ojs_demos.description: Fair random walk. Define stopping rule: stop when X hits a or -b. Run 10,000 simulations. Verify E(X_τ) = 0 = X_0. Show P(hitting a) = b/(a+b). Gambler's ruin from martingale perspective.

ojs_demos.title: Sequential Probability Ratio Test
ojs_demos.description: SPRT for testing H₀: p=p₀ vs H₁: p=p₁. Plot log-likelihood ratio as data arrives. Show upper and lower boundaries (Wald). Stop when boundary crossed. Show ASN (average sample number) < fixed-sample test.

chapters_advanced.title: Ch52: Bayesian Probability — Theory and Computation
content: 52.1 Bayesian framework — prior × likelihood = posterior (up to normalizing constant)
52.2 Conjugate families — Beta-Binomial, Normal-Normal, Gamma-Poisson, Dirichlet-Multinomial
52.3 Non-conjugate models — when closed-form posteriors don't exist
52.4 Loss functions and Bayesian decision theory — squared error, 0-1, absolute
52.5 Bayesian point estimation — posterior mean, MAP, posterior median
52.6 Credible intervals — highest posterior density (HPD) vs equal-tailed
52.7 Bayesian hypothesis testing — Bayes factors, posterior odds
52.8 Prior elicitation — informative, weakly informative, reference priors, Jeffreys' prior
52.9 Hierarchical models — priors on priors, shrinkage, borrowing strength
52.10 Clinical: Bayesian clinical trial design, adaptive trials, prior specification for medical devices (FDA guidance)

ojs_demos.title: Conjugate Prior Laboratory
ojs_demos.description: Dropdown: Beta-Binomial, Normal-Normal, Gamma-Poisson. Set prior parameters. Add data one observation at a time. Watch posterior update in real-time. Show prior → posterior → next prior chain. Compute posterior mean, credible interval, Bayes factor. THE comprehensive Bayesian demo.

ojs_demos.title: Prior Sensitivity Analysis
ojs_demos.description: Same likelihood. Three different priors (informative, weakly informative, diffuse). Show three posteriors. As n increases, all converge. Show when prior matters (small n) vs when it doesn't (large n). Compute Bayes factor sensitivity.

ojs_demos.title: HPD vs Equal-Tailed Interval
ojs_demos.description: Skewed posterior. Show both interval types. HPD is shorter. Equal-tailed is easier to compute. Slider: credibility level (0.50 to 0.99). Show both intervals on the posterior density.

ojs_demos.title: Bayes Factor Calculator
ojs_demos.description: Two models M₁ and M₂. Input data. Compute marginal likelihood for each. BF = p(data|M₁)/p(data|M₂). Show Jeffreys' scale of evidence. Compare to p-value for same data.

ojs_demos.title: Hierarchical Model Shrinkage
ojs_demos.description: Multiple groups with different sample sizes. Show raw group means. Show hierarchical Bayesian estimates (shrunk toward grand mean). Small groups shrink more. Visualize the bias-variance tradeoff of shrinkage.

chapters_advanced.webr_analysis: Upload data → specify prior family → compute posterior → credible intervals → Bayes factor → prior sensitivity check → report

chapters_advanced.title: Ch53: Information Theory and Entropy
content: 53.1 Shannon entropy — H(X) = -Σ p(x) log p(x), uncertainty quantified
53.2 Properties: non-negativity, maximized by Uniform, bits vs nats
53.3 Joint entropy, conditional entropy — H(X,Y), H(Y|X)
53.4 Mutual information — I(X;Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y), shared information
53.5 KL divergence — D_{KL}(P||Q), not symmetric, always ≥ 0, connection to likelihood ratio
53.6 Cross-entropy — H(P,Q), connection to log-loss in machine learning
53.7 Fisher information — I(θ) = E[(d/dθ log f(X|θ))²], precision of estimation
53.8 Cramér-Rao lower bound — Var(θ̂) ≥ 1/I(θ), best possible precision
53.9 Clinical: entropy of diagnostic tests, information gain from additional tests, AIC/BIC as information criteria

ojs_demos.title: Entropy Calculator
ojs_demos.description: Input discrete probability distribution (bar heights). Compute H(X). Show contribution of each outcome: -p log p. Most uncertain = highest entropy = Uniform. Slider: adjust probabilities, watch entropy change.

ojs_demos.title: Mutual Information Venn Diagram
ojs_demos.description: H(X) and H(Y) as circles. Overlap = I(X;Y). H(X,Y) = total area. H(X|Y) = H(X) - I(X;Y). Adjust joint distribution, watch Venn diagram change. Independent: circles don't overlap.

ojs_demos.title: KL Divergence Visualizer
ojs_demos.description: Two distributions P and Q. Show both PDFs. Compute D_KL(P||Q) and D_KL(Q||P) — show they're different (not symmetric). Show pointwise contribution: p(x) log(p(x)/q(x)). Clinical: how different is this patient population from the trial population?

ojs_demos.title: Fisher Information and CRLB
ojs_demos.description: Select distribution family (Binomial, Normal, Poisson). Parameter slider. Compute Fisher information I(θ). Show Cramér-Rao bound: best any unbiased estimator can do. Compare MLE variance to CRLB. Show: MLE achieves CRLB (is efficient) for exponential families.

chapters_advanced.title: Ch54: Maximum Likelihood and Estimation Theory
content: 54.1 Point estimation — estimators vs estimates, properties (unbiased, consistent, efficient)
54.2 Method of moments — match sample moments to distribution moments
54.3 Maximum likelihood — L(θ|x), log-likelihood, score equation
54.4 Properties of MLE — consistency, asymptotic normality, efficiency, invariance
54.5 MLE for common distributions — Normal, Binomial, Poisson, Exponential, Gamma
54.6 Confidence intervals from MLE — Wald, profile likelihood, likelihood ratio
54.7 EM algorithm — when data is incomplete, E-step/M-step, convergence
54.8 Sufficient statistics — Rao-Blackwell, Lehmann-Scheffé, MVUE
54.9 Clinical: fitting distributions to clinical data, mixture models for heterogeneous populations, MLE in survival analysis

ojs_demos.title: Likelihood Function Explorer
ojs_demos.description: Observe data (adjustable). Select distribution family. Plot L(θ) or log L(θ) vs θ. Mark MLE (peak). Show profile likelihood CI. Compare to Wald CI. Animate: add data points one at a time, watch likelihood sharpen.

ojs_demos.title: MLE for Normal Distribution
ojs_demos.description: Generate data from N(μ,σ²). Show log-likelihood surface over (μ,σ²) space. Contour plot. Mark MLE at peak. Show that μ̂ = x̄ and σ̂² = (1/n)Σ(xᵢ-x̄)² (biased!). Compare to s² (unbiased). Show asymptotic normality of MLE.

ojs_demos.title: EM Algorithm Step-by-Step
ojs_demos.description: Mixture of two Normals. Data is unlabeled. E-step: compute responsibilities (soft assignments). M-step: update parameters. Animate iteration by iteration. Watch log-likelihood increase monotonically. Show convergence.

ojs_demos.title: Method of Moments vs MLE
ojs_demos.description: Same data. Fit with MoM and MLE. Compare estimates. Show that MLE is more efficient (smaller variance). Demonstrate with Gamma distribution where difference is visible.

chapters_advanced.webr_analysis: Upload data → fit distributions via MLE and MoM → compare AIC/BIC → profile likelihood CIs → goodness-of-fit diagnostics → EM for mixture models

chapters_expert.title: Ch55: Brownian Motion and Diffusion
content: 55.1 Brownian motion as limit of random walks — Donsker's theorem
55.2 Properties: continuous paths, independent increments, Normal increments, nowhere differentiable
55.3 Brownian motion as martingale — E(W(t)|F_s) = W(s)
55.4 Geometric Brownian motion — dS = μS dt + σS dW, stock prices, biomarker trajectories
55.5 First passage times — distribution, connection to inverse Gaussian
55.6 Brownian bridge — conditioned to return to origin, connection to goodness-of-fit (KS test)
55.7 Clinical: continuous biomarker monitoring, pharmacokinetic models, diffusion MRI

ojs_demos.title: Brownian Motion Simulator
ojs_demos.description: Animate Brownian paths. Show 10 simultaneous paths. Verify properties: E(W(t))=0, Var(W(t))=t. Zoom in: paths are self-similar. Show that |W(t+h)-W(t)|/√h → N(0,1).

ojs_demos.title: Random Walk to Brownian Motion
ojs_demos.description: Start with discrete random walk. Increase step frequency and decrease step size simultaneously (Donsker scaling). Watch discrete path converge to continuous Brownian path. Animated transition.

ojs_demos.title: Geometric Brownian Motion
ojs_demos.description: Model asset price: dS/S = μdt + σdW. Sliders: μ (drift), σ (volatility). Generate paths. Show log-normal distribution of S(t). Compute P(S(t) > threshold). Clinical: tumor growth modeling.

chapters_expert.title: Ch56: Monte Carlo Methods
content: 56.1 Simple Monte Carlo integration — E[g(X)] ≈ (1/n)Σg(X_i)
56.2 Variance reduction: importance sampling — weight by proposal/target ratio
56.3 Variance reduction: antithetic variates — negative correlation trick
56.4 Variance reduction: control variates — use known expectations
56.5 Variance reduction: stratified sampling — partition and sample each stratum
56.6 Markov Chain Monte Carlo — Metropolis-Hastings algorithm
56.7 Gibbs sampling — special case of MH for conditional distributions
56.8 MCMC diagnostics — trace plots, autocorrelation, R-hat, ESS, burn-in
56.9 Hamiltonian Monte Carlo — NUTS (the algorithm behind Stan/Turing.jl)
56.10 Clinical: MCMC for Bayesian clinical trials, pharmacokinetic modeling, complex hierarchical models

ojs_demos.title: Monte Carlo Integration
ojs_demos.description: Estimate ∫g(x)f(x)dx by sampling. Show function, samples, running average. Convergence plot with CI band narrowing as n grows. Compare: simple MC, importance sampling, control variates. Show variance reduction ratios.

ojs_demos.title: Metropolis-Hastings Visualizer
ojs_demos.description: 2D target distribution (e.g., banana-shaped, bimodal). Show proposal distribution. Animate: propose, accept/reject, move/stay. Show trace plots, burn-in, mixing. Autocorrelation. Tune proposal variance — too small (slow mixing) vs too large (many rejections). THE MCMC demo.

ojs_demos.title: Gibbs Sampler
ojs_demos.description: Bivariate target. Alternate: sample X|Y, then Y|X. Animate the zig-zag path. Show marginal histograms building up. Compare to direct Metropolis-Hastings on same target.

ojs_demos.title: MCMC Diagnostics Dashboard
ojs_demos.description: Run MCMC chain. Show: trace plot, ACF plot, running mean, R-hat (with multiple chains), ESS. Traffic light system: green (converged), yellow (questionable), red (not converged). Recommend: more iterations, thinning, or reparameterization.

ojs_demos.title: Importance Sampling
ojs_demos.description: Estimate P(rare event) via importance sampling. Show: original distribution (almost no samples in tail), proposal distribution (concentrated in tail), weights, weighted estimate. Compare efficiency to naive MC. Clinical: rare adverse event probability estimation.

chapters_expert.webr_analysis: Specify Bayesian model → run MCMC via R → full diagnostics → posterior summaries → credible intervals → model comparison via WAIC/LOO

chapters_expert.title: Ch57: Random Graphs and Network Probability
content: 57.1 Erdős-Rényi random graphs — G(n,p), phase transition at p = 1/n
57.2 Degree distribution — Binomial for ER, power-law for scale-free
57.3 Small-world networks — Watts-Strogatz model, clustering + short paths
57.4 Scale-free networks — Barabási-Albert preferential attachment
57.5 Epidemic models on networks — SIR/SIS, basic reproduction number R₀
57.6 Percolation — threshold for giant component, bond vs site percolation
57.7 Clinical: disease transmission networks, hospital contact networks, social determinants, genomic networks

ojs_demos.title: Random Graph Explorer
ojs_demos.description: Generate G(n,p). Slider: n (5-200), p (0-1). Visualize graph (force-directed layout). Show degree distribution. Highlight giant component. Show phase transition at p ≈ 1/n: sudden emergence of giant component.

ojs_demos.title: Epidemic Simulator on Network
ojs_demos.description: SIR model on a random graph. Patient zero infects neighbors with probability β. Recovery rate γ. R₀ = β/γ × average degree. Animate spread. Show epidemic curve (new infections vs time). Compare ER vs scale-free networks.

ojs_demos.title: Small World Demo
ojs_demos.description: Start with ring lattice. Slider: rewiring probability p. Show clustering coefficient and average path length vs p. The 'sweet spot': high clustering + short paths. Six degrees of separation.

chapters_expert.title: Ch58: Probability in Machine Learning
content: 58.1 Probabilistic classification — Bayes optimal classifier, Naive Bayes
58.2 Logistic regression as probabilistic model — MLE, connection to exponential family
58.3 Gaussian processes — infinite-dimensional Normal, non-parametric regression
58.4 Expectation-Maximization — full treatment with convergence proof
58.5 Variational inference — ELBO, mean-field approximation
58.6 PAC learning — probably approximately correct, VC dimension
58.7 Concentration inequalities in ML — Hoeffding, McDiarmid, matrix concentration
58.8 Clinical: probabilistic diagnosis, calibrated prediction, uncertainty quantification

ojs_demos.title: Naive Bayes Classifier
ojs_demos.description: Two classes, two features. Show class-conditional distributions. Compute posteriors. Decision boundary. Add data points, watch boundary shift. Show when 'naive' assumption fails.

ojs_demos.title: Gaussian Process Demo
ojs_demos.description: Prior: sample functions from GP (mean=0, kernel=RBF). Observe data points. Posterior: sample functions conditioned on data. Show mean function + uncertainty band. Slider: kernel length scale. Clinical: smooth biomarker trajectory estimation.

ojs_demos.title: Calibration and Uncertainty
ojs_demos.description: Train a model. Show reliability diagram (predicted probability vs observed frequency). Compute ECE (expected calibration error). Apply Platt scaling or isotonic regression. Show before/after calibration.

chapters_expert.title: Ch59: Measure-Theoretic Probability — Essential Concepts
content: 59.1 Why measure theory? — limitations of elementary probability for continuous/infinite cases
59.2 σ-algebras — information, filtrations, measurability (intuitive treatment)
59.3 Probability measures — Kolmogorov axioms formalized
59.4 Random variables as measurable functions — unifying discrete and continuous
59.5 Lebesgue integral — E(X) = ∫X dP, why it generalizes both sums and Riemann integrals
59.6 Product spaces and Fubini's theorem — joint distributions justified
59.7 Radon-Nikodym theorem — densities as derivatives of measures
59.8 Conditional expectation as projection — E(Y|G) in Hilbert space, best L² predictor
59.9 Why this matters for applied researchers — understanding assumptions behind every test, when methods break down

ojs_demos.title: σ-Algebra Information Demo
ojs_demos.description: Finite sample space Ω. Show all possible σ-algebras (coarsest to finest). Demonstrate: more information = finer σ-algebra = more events distinguishable. Filtration: σ-algebras growing over time as information arrives.

ojs_demos.title: Riemann vs Lebesgue Integration
ojs_demos.description: Pathological function (e.g., Dirichlet function). Riemann integral fails. Lebesgue integral works by partitioning the range instead of the domain. Visual: horizontal slicing vs vertical slicing.

ojs_demos.title: Conditional Expectation as Projection
ojs_demos.description: L² space of random variables. E(Y|X) is the projection of Y onto the space of X-measurable functions. Visual: 3D vector analogy. Show that Y - E(Y|X) is orthogonal to X-measurable functions (prediction residual is uncorrelated with predictor).

chapters_expert.title: Ch60: Capstone — Probability in Clinical Decision Making
content: 60.1 The probabilistic clinician — every diagnosis is a probability, every treatment is a gamble
60.2 Decision trees and expected utility — structuring clinical decisions
60.3 Value of information — EVPI, EVSI, when is an additional test worth doing?
60.4 Threshold analysis — treatment threshold, test threshold, test-treatment threshold
60.5 Sequential decision making under uncertainty — dynamic programming, POMDP basics
60.6 Probabilistic sensitivity analysis — uncertainty propagation through decision models
60.7 Calibration and clinical judgment — are physicians well-calibrated probabilistic thinkers?
60.8 Communicating probability to patients — absolute vs relative risk, natural frequencies, icon arrays
60.9 The future: probabilistic AI in clinical decision support
60.10 Master probability concept map — connecting all 30 chapters

ojs_demos.title: Clinical Decision Tree Builder
ojs_demos.description: Drag-and-drop: decision nodes (squares), chance nodes (circles), terminal nodes (triangles). Input probabilities and utilities. Fold back the tree. Show optimal strategy. One-way and two-way sensitivity analysis. Tornado diagram.

ojs_demos.title: Value of Information Calculator
ojs_demos.description: Base case decision. Compute EVPI: maximum you'd pay for perfect information. Compute EVSI for a specific diagnostic test. Show: if EVSI < test cost → don't test. Interactive sliders for test accuracy and disease prevalence.

ojs_demos.title: Threshold Analysis
ojs_demos.description: Treatment threshold P_t: treat if P(disease) > P_t. Test threshold P_{test}: test if P(disease) is between P_{test-} and P_{test+}. Sliders: treatment benefit/harm ratio, test sensitivity/specificity. Show optimal strategy zones.

ojs_demos.title: Risk Communication Workshop
ojs_demos.description: Same risk expressed as: absolute risk, relative risk, OR, NNT, natural frequency (1 in X), icon array. Let user toggle between representations. Quiz: which representation do patients understand best? Evidence from health literacy research.

ojs_demos.title: Master Concept Map
ojs_demos.description: Interactive D3 force-directed graph connecting all probability concepts from Ch31-60. Click any node to navigate to that chapter. Color by tier. Show prerequisite paths. Search by topic. THE capstone integrative visualization.

chapters_expert.webr_analysis: Upload clinical scenario parameters → build decision tree → compute expected utility → sensitivity analysis → optimal strategy report → patient communication materials
